title: Memory for Machines — Embeddings, Vector Databases and RAG
subtitle: How to give an AI your knowledge without ever retraining it
theme: Turn meaning into geometry, store it, retrieve it, and let the model answer from YOUR truth.
learningObjectives: Explain what an embedding really is — meaning as coordinates in a high-dimensional space
Understand vector similarity (cosine) and how nearest-neighbor search finds relevant text
Compare vector databases (FAISS, pgvector, Pinecone-style) and when to use each
Master chunking — the unglamorous step that makes or breaks a RAG system
Trace a full RAG pipeline end to end, from a user question to a grounded, cited answer
Diagnose RAG failure modes and evaluate a RAG system with real metrics
storyHook: A model trained in 2024 cannot know what your company decided yesterday, what your 900-page contract says, or what your patient's last lab result was. Retraining it would cost a fortune and be obsolete by Friday. So in 2023 a deceptively simple idea swept the enterprise world: don't change the model's brain — hand it the right page at the right moment, and ask it to read. That idea is RAG, and as of June 2026 it is the single most deployed AI pattern on Earth.

slides.title: The Brilliant Intern With No Memory
slides.body:
## The problem RAG was born to solve

Imagine hiring the most brilliant intern alive. They've read most of the internet, write flawless code, reason through hard problems, and speak forty languages. There's just one catch: **they have no access to your files, no memory of your company, and their knowledge froze on the day they were hired.**

Ask them 'What's our refund policy?' and they'll either say 'I don't know' (if you're lucky) or **invent a plausible-sounding policy** (if you're not). That invented policy is what we call a *hallucination*, and it's the number-one reason naked LLMs are dangerous in the enterprise.

You have two ways to fix this:

1. **Send the intern back to school** for a year to memorize your files — expensive, slow, and stale the moment a file changes. *(That's fine-tuning — yesterday's topic.)*
2. **Give the intern a really good filing cabinet and a search assistant** — so that every time you ask a question, they instantly pull the three most relevant pages, read them, and answer *from the documents in front of them.*

That second option is **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)**. It's cheaper, it's always current, and — crucially — the answer is **grounded in sources you can check.**

Today we build that filing cabinet, that search assistant, and the whole machine around them. By the end you'll understand why nearly every serious enterprise AI product in 2026 has RAG at its core.
slides.speakerNotes: Open with the intern story and let it breathe — it's the spine of the whole day. The key emotional beat: hallucination is the enemy, grounding is the cure. Ask the class: 'Who here has caught ChatGPT confidently making something up?' Hands will go up. Say: 'Today we fix exactly that — by giving it the right page before it answers.'
slides.marketConnection: As of June 2026, RAG is the default architecture for enterprise AI assistants — customer support, internal knowledge bots, legal and medical copilots — precisely because it delivers current, source-grounded answers without the cost and staleness of retraining.

slides.title: What Is an Embedding, Really?
slides.body:
## Turning meaning into numbers

Computers can't compare *meaning* — they compare *numbers*. So the foundational trick of RAG is to convert a piece of text into a list of numbers that **captures what it means.** That list is called an **embedding**.

```
"a small dog"  ->  [0.12, -0.88, 0.41, ... , 0.07]   (e.g. 1,536 numbers)
```

Think of each text as a **point in a vast, high-dimensional space** — not 2 or 3 dimensions, but hundreds or thousands. The magic property:

> **Texts with similar meaning land close together. Texts with different meaning land far apart.**

### The mental picture: a map of meaning

Imagine a giant map where every concept has a location:

```
        cat  dog
          \  /
         pets        (cluster of animal/pet meanings)
          ...
   ...........................
          ...
        invoice  receipt
           \    /
          billing   (cluster of finance meanings)
```

'Cat' and 'dog' sit near each other. 'Invoice' and 'receipt' cluster on the other side of town. The model learned these positions from reading billions of sentences — it noticed which words and ideas show up in similar contexts.

### The famous party trick

Embeddings encode *relationships*, not just topics:

```
vector("king") - vector("man") + vector("woman")  ≈  vector("queen")
```

The direction from 'man' to 'woman' is roughly the same direction as from 'king' to 'queen'. Meaning has **geometry.**

### Why this is the whole foundation

If I embed your question and embed every paragraph in your knowledge base, then **'find the relevant paragraphs' becomes 'find the nearest points.'** Search is now geometry. That single shift is what makes RAG possible.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the conceptual keystone — go slow. Draw the meaning-map on the whiteboard live. The king-man+woman=queen example always lands; it makes 'meaning has geometry' click. Ask: 'If I embed the question "how do I get my money back?" near which cluster does it land — pets or billing?' (Billing — even though it shares no words with "refund policy.") That's the superpower: semantic, not keyword.
slides.marketConnection: Embedding models (OpenAI, Cohere, Voyage, and strong open-source options) are a mature, cheap commodity in 2026 — high-quality embeddings cost a tiny fraction of generation, which is why semantic search now underpins everything from support tools to code assistants.

slides.title: Measuring Closeness — Cosine Similarity
slides.body:
## How do we know two points are 'close'?

Once every text is a point (a vector), we need a way to measure how similar two of them are. The workhorse is **cosine similarity** — it measures the **angle** between two vectors, not the distance.

```
  cos similarity = 1.0   ->  same direction   ->  same meaning
  cos similarity = 0.0   ->  perpendicular    ->  unrelated
  cos similarity = -1.0  ->  opposite         ->  opposite meaning
```

### Why angle, not raw distance?

Because we care about **direction of meaning**, not magnitude. A short sentence and a long paragraph about the same topic point the *same way* even if their vectors have different lengths. Cosine ignores length and asks: 'Are these pointing toward the same idea?'

### A tiny intuition in 2D

```
          ^ "refund"
         /
        /  small angle = very similar
       /  /
      /  / "money back"
     / /
    /_/_________________> "weather forecast" (big angle = unrelated)
```

'Refund' and 'money back' point nearly the same way (high cosine). 'Weather forecast' points off at a wide angle (low cosine).

### From one comparison to a search

To answer a question, we:
1. Embed the question into a vector.
2. Compute its similarity to **every** chunk in the knowledge base.
3. Return the **top-k** most similar chunks (e.g. the top 5).

Doing this against millions of vectors, fast, is exactly the job of a **vector database** — our next slide. The naive approach (compare to every single vector) is called **brute-force / exact search**; at scale we use clever **approximate nearest neighbor (ANN)** algorithms that trade a sliver of accuracy for enormous speed.
slides.speakerNotes: Don't get lost in trigonometry. The takeaway: cosine = 'are these pointing the same way?', 1 is identical meaning, 0 is unrelated. Emphasize that we return TOP-K, not just the single best — RAG usually feeds the model several chunks. Ask: 'Why might we want the top 5 chunks instead of just the top 1?' (One chunk may miss part of the answer; redundancy and coverage.)
slides.marketConnection: The shift from keyword search (matching words) to semantic search (matching meaning via embeddings + cosine) is one of the most quietly transformative changes in enterprise software over 2023-2026.

slides.title: The Vector Database — A Library Built for Meaning
slides.body:
## Where the embeddings live

A **vector database** does one job superbly: store millions of vectors and, given a query vector, return the nearest ones in **milliseconds**. Comparing against every vector one-by-one (brute force) is fine for thousands of items but collapses at millions — so vector DBs use **Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN)** indexes (like HNSW) that navigate the space cleverly instead of checking everything.

```
        QUERY VECTOR
             │
             ▼
   ┌──────────────────────┐
   │   VECTOR DATABASE     │
   │  ┌────────────────┐   │
   │  │ ANN index(HNSW)│   │  <- 'shortcuts' through the space
   │  └────────────────┘   │
   │   millions of vectors │
   └──────────┬───────────┘
              ▼
     top-k nearest chunks  (+ their original text & metadata)
```

### The three flavors you'll meet in the wild

| Option | What it is | Best when |
|---|---|---|
| **FAISS** | A blazing-fast open-source *library* (from Meta) for similarity search. Runs in your process. | Prototyping, research, full control, you manage everything yourself. |
| **pgvector** | An extension that adds vector search to **PostgreSQL**. | You already run Postgres and want vectors *next to* your relational data — one database, one backup, SQL filters. |
| **Pinecone / managed (Weaviate, Qdrant, Milvus...)** | Fully managed, scalable vector DB *services*. | Production scale, you'd rather not run the infra, need replication/uptime out of the box. |

### The detail beginners miss: metadata + filtering

A good vector DB stores more than the vector. Each entry carries the **original text** and **metadata** (source, date, author, department, access level). This lets you do **hybrid queries**: 'find semantically similar chunks *where department = legal AND date > 2025*.' Metadata filtering is what makes RAG safe and precise in the enterprise — you can enforce permissions and freshness right in the retrieval step.
slides.speakerNotes: Keep the three-flavor table front and center — it's the practical decision people actually face. The single most useful real-world tip on this slide is metadata filtering: it's how you do access control and freshness. Ask: 'If a junior employee asks the bot a question, how do we stop it from retrieving an executives-only document?' (Metadata filter on access level — done in retrieval, before the model ever sees it.)
slides.marketConnection: In June 2026, pgvector has become the pragmatic default for teams already on Postgres ('just add vectors to the DB you have'), while managed services like Pinecone, Qdrant, and Weaviate dominate large-scale or low-ops deployments — and FAISS remains the go-to for prototyping and research.

slides.title: Chunking — The Unglamorous Step That Decides Everything
slides.body:
## You can't embed a 400-page PDF as one blob

Embedding models work on bite-sized passages, not whole books. So before anything else, you must **split your documents into chunks** — and this boring step is, in practice, **the single biggest determinant of RAG quality.** Get chunking wrong and the world's best model retrieves garbage.

### Why chunk size is a Goldilocks problem

```
CHUNKS TOO BIG:                CHUNKS TOO SMALL:
- one chunk covers many        - a single fact gets split
  topics                         across two chunks
- the relevant sentence is     - chunk lacks context to
  diluted by noise               be meaningful on its own
- you waste the model's        - retrieval returns fragments
  context window                 that don't answer anything
```

The sweet spot is usually a few hundred tokens per chunk — big enough to hold a complete idea, small enough to stay focused.

### The pro techniques

- **Overlap** — let consecutive chunks share some text (e.g. 10-20% overlap) so a sentence split across a boundary still appears whole in at least one chunk.
- **Semantic / structure-aware chunking** — split on natural boundaries (headings, paragraphs, sections) instead of blindly every N characters. A chunk that respects a section heading carries its own context.
- **Keep metadata with each chunk** — source file, page, section title. This powers citations *and* lets the model (and the human) know where the answer came from.
- **Don't shred tables and code** — these need structure-aware handling or they become meaningless soup.

### The rule to tattoo on your arm

> **A chunk should be a self-contained, meaningful unit of thought — something that, read alone, makes sense and could answer a question.**

Most 'RAG doesn't work for us' complaints trace back to lazy chunking. This is where the engineering craft lives.
slides.speakerNotes: Sell how UNDERrated this is: 'Everyone obsesses over the fancy model and ignores chunking — which is where 80% of RAG failures actually come from.' Use a vivid example: imagine a contract clause split mid-sentence across two chunks so neither retrieves correctly. Ask: 'If a key definition lives in section 1 but the obligation that uses it is in section 40, how does the model connect them?' (It often can't — which previews retrieval failure modes and why metadata/structure matter.)
slides.marketConnection: In 2026 there's a small industry around document parsing and intelligent chunking (handling PDFs, tables, and layout), because teams learned the hard way that retrieval quality is bottlenecked by ingestion quality, not by the LLM.

slides.title: RAG, End to End — The Whole Machine
slides.body:
## Two phases: build the library, then answer questions

RAG has a one-time **ingestion** phase and a per-query **retrieval + generation** phase. Here's the entire pipeline.

### Phase 1 — Ingestion (do this once, refresh as docs change)

```
  Your documents (PDFs, wikis, tickets, DBs)
            │
            ▼
     [1] CHUNK them into meaningful passages
            │
            ▼
     [2] EMBED each chunk -> a vector
            │
            ▼
     [3] STORE vectors + text + metadata in the VECTOR DB
```

### Phase 2 — Query time (every time a user asks something)

```
  User question: "What's our refund window for EU orders?"
            │
            ▼
     [4] EMBED the question -> query vector
            │
            ▼
     [5] RETRIEVE top-k nearest chunks from the vector DB
         (optionally filtered: region=EU, type=policy)
            │
            ▼
     [6] AUGMENT: build a prompt =
         "Using ONLY the context below, answer the question.
          Context: <chunk1> <chunk2> <chunk3>
          Question: What's our refund window for EU orders?"
            │
            ▼
     [7] GENERATE: the LLM answers, GROUNDED in the chunks
            │
            ▼
     [8] CITE: return the answer + which sources it used
```

### The single most important line in the whole system

> **'Using ONLY the context below...'**

That instruction is what transforms a guessing machine into a grounded one. It tells the model: *don't reach into your frozen memory — answer from these documents, and if the answer isn't here, say so.* This is the difference between a confident hallucination and 'I don't have that information in the provided sources.'

Notice what we **never** did: we never retrained the model. We just got very good at handing it the right page.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the centerpiece slide — walk all 8 steps in order, twice if needed. Make them see the two phases clearly (ingest once, query many times). Then put a spotlight on step 6's 'Using ONLY the context below' — read it aloud and say 'this sentence is worth more than most of the code.' Ask: 'What should the model do if the answer isn't in the retrieved chunks?' (Say it doesn't know — and that's a FEATURE, not a bug.)
slides.marketConnection: This exact 8-step pipeline is the backbone of thousands of production AI products in 2026; frameworks like LangChain and LlamaIndex exist to wire it together, but the conceptual flow is identical everywhere and is now considered foundational AI engineering knowledge.

slides.title: Why RAG Beats Fine-Tuning for Knowledge
slides.body:
## Settling yesterday's cliffhanger

On Day 10 we drew the decision triangle and said: **knowledge → RAG, behavior → fine-tune.** Now you can feel *why* in your bones.

### Head to head, for the specific job of 'knowing facts'

| Dimension | RAG | Fine-tuning |
|---|---|---|
| **Freshness** | Update a document, re-embed it — live in seconds. | Knowledge is frozen at training time; new facts need a new training run. |
| **Citations / trust** | Returns *which source* it used — auditable, verifiable. | The fact is dissolved into weights; you can't point to a source. |
| **Cost to update** | Cheap — just ingest the changed docs. | Expensive — re-run training to add/correct knowledge. |
| **Hallucination control** | High — 'answer only from context; else say you don't know.' | Lower — the model still guesses from blended memory. |
| **Access control** | Filter by permissions at retrieval time. | Once baked in, everyone who can query can surface it. |
| **Correcting a mistake** | Edit/delete one document. | Re-train to unlearn — genuinely hard. |

### The decisive trio: freshness, traceability, control

Enterprises don't just want a *right* answer — they want a **defensible, current, permission-aware** answer they can audit. RAG delivers exactly that. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge into a black box that goes stale and can't be cited.

### So when DOES fine-tuning still earn its place?

For **behavior**: a consistent tone, a strict output format, a domain *style*. The dream architecture combines both: **RAG supplies the facts, a LoRA adapter shapes the delivery.** Knowledge and behavior, each handled by the right tool.

> **RAG changes what the model *can see*. Fine-tuning changes how the model *behaves*. Knowledge is a 'see' problem.**
slides.speakerNotes: This slide pays off Day 10 — call back to the triangle explicitly. The three killer words are freshness, traceability (citations), and access control. In regulated industries, citations alone are the deciding factor. Ask: 'A hospital wants an AI that answers from the latest treatment guidelines AND can prove which guideline it cited. RAG or fine-tune?' (RAG — and the citation requirement makes it non-negotiable.)
slides.marketConnection: The 'RAG for knowledge, fine-tune for behavior' split is settled best practice in 2026 — and in regulated sectors (finance, healthcare, legal), RAG's auditability and citations are often a hard compliance requirement, not a nice-to-have.

slides.title: When RAG Goes Wrong — The Failure Modes
slides.body:
## RAG is not magic — it fails in specific, diagnosable ways

A team ships a RAG bot, demos beautifully, then real users hit it and the answers get weird. Here's the rogues' gallery — and crucially, *where in the pipeline each fault lives.* Most RAG debugging is figuring out: **did retrieval fail, or did generation fail?**

### Retrieval-side failures (the right chunk never arrived)

- **Bad chunking** — the answer was split across chunks, so no single chunk retrieves well. *(Fix: better chunking + overlap.)*
- **Semantic mismatch** — the user's wording is far from the document's wording in embedding space, so the right chunk ranks low. *(Fix: query rewriting, hybrid keyword+vector search, better embeddings.)*
- **The needle is buried** — top-k is too small, or too many near-duplicates crowd out the real answer. *(Fix: tune k, deduplicate, add a reranker.)*
- **Stale or missing data** — the document simply isn't in the index. RAG can't retrieve what you never ingested.

### Generation-side failures (the right chunk arrived, the answer still went bad)

- **Ignored context** — the model answers from its own memory instead of the chunks. *(Fix: stronger 'use ONLY the context' instruction.)*
- **Lost in the middle** — with many chunks, models can overlook info buried in the middle of a long context. *(Fix: rerank so the best chunk is near the top; send fewer, better chunks.)*
- **Hallucinated citation** — it cites a source that doesn't actually support the claim. *(Fix: verification step; cite-then-check.)*

### The reranker — the secret weapon

A common upgrade: retrieve a generous top-20 with the (fast, cheap) embedding search, then use a slower, smarter **reranker model** to re-score and keep the best 3-5. Retrieval casts a wide net; the reranker picks the keepers. This one addition fixes a huge fraction of 'the right doc was there but ranked 7th' failures.
slides.speakerNotes: The master skill here is the diagnostic split: RETRIEVAL failure vs GENERATION failure. Teach them to always ask first: 'Was the right chunk even retrieved?' If you can't answer that, you can't debug. Introduce reranking as the highest-ROI upgrade. Tell a quick war story of a bot that 'ignored the docs' — turned out retrieval was fine but the prompt was weak. Ask: 'How would you even check whether retrieval or generation failed?' (Log the retrieved chunks and look.)
slides.marketConnection: Rerankers (Cohere Rerank, cross-encoders) and hybrid search are standard production upgrades in 2026; the maturing discipline of 'RAG engineering' is largely about systematically diagnosing and fixing these exact retrieval-vs-generation failure modes.

slides.title: How Do You Know It's Actually Working? — Evaluating RAG
slides.body:
## 'It looked good in the demo' is not an evaluation

RAG has two stages, so it has two things to measure. Evaluate them **separately** — because a great answer can hide bad retrieval, and great retrieval can be wrecked by bad generation.

### Evaluate retrieval (did we fetch the right stuff?)

- **Context Recall** — of the chunks needed to answer, how many did we retrieve? (Did the right page show up at all?)
- **Context Precision** — of the chunks we retrieved, how many were actually relevant? (How much noise did we drag in?)
- These mirror classic search metrics (recall@k, precision@k). If recall is low, your *retrieval* is the bottleneck — no prompt tweak will save you.

### Evaluate generation (given the chunks, was the answer good?)

- **Faithfulness / Groundedness** — is every claim in the answer actually supported by the retrieved context? (This is the anti-hallucination metric — the one that matters most.)
- **Answer Relevance** — does the answer actually address the user's question?
- **Correctness** — does it match the known right answer (when you have a labeled set)?

### How teams measure this in practice

- **A golden test set** — a curated set of (question, ideal-answer, source-chunks) you score against on every change. Non-negotiable.
- **LLM-as-a-judge** — use a strong model to score faithfulness and relevance at scale. Imperfect, but vastly better than vibes, and the dominant approach in 2026.
- **Frameworks** — tools like RAGAS, TruLens, and others operationalize exactly these metrics so you can track them like a dashboard.

### The mindset shift

> **A RAG system is a product, not a prompt. You ship it with a test suite, watch faithfulness and recall over time, and treat a drop in groundedness like a production bug — because it is.**
slides.speakerNotes: The big idea: evaluate the two stages SEPARATELY. Drill the diagnostic logic: low context recall => fix retrieval; high recall but low faithfulness => fix generation/prompting. Faithfulness is the metric to obsess over — it's the hallucination meter. Mention LLM-as-judge plainly: 'We literally use one AI to grade another's homework, against a golden set.' Ask: 'If faithfulness is high but answer-relevance is low, what's wrong?' (It's grounded but answering the wrong question — maybe query understanding or chunk selection.)
slides.marketConnection: RAG evaluation went from afterthought to core discipline by 2026 — frameworks like RAGAS and TruLens plus LLM-as-judge pipelines are standard, and 'what's your faithfulness score?' is now a normal question in enterprise AI procurement and audits.

slides.title: A RAG Pipeline in ~30 Lines
slides.body:
## The whole machine, small enough to read in one sitting

Stripped to its essence so the *shape* is undeniable. Real systems add chunking sophistication, reranking, and eval — but this is the beating heart.

```python
# ---------- PHASE 1: INGEST (run once) ----------
chunks = chunk_documents(my_docs, size=400, overlap=60)   # slide 5

vectors = embed(chunks)            # text -> coordinates in meaning-space
vector_db.add(vectors, chunks, metadata)   # store vector + text + source

# ---------- PHASE 2: ANSWER (per question) ----------
def answer(question):
    # [4] embed the question into the same meaning-space
    q_vec = embed([question])[0]

    # [5] retrieve the nearest chunks (optionally filtered by metadata)
    hits = vector_db.search(q_vec, top_k=5, filter={"lang": "en"})

    # (optional but high-ROI) [5b] rerank to keep the best 3
    hits = rerank(question, hits)[:3]

    # [6] AUGMENT — the prompt that grounds the model
    context = "\n\n".join(h.text for h in hits)
    prompt = f"""Answer using ONLY the context below.
If the answer is not in the context, say you don't know.

Context:
{context}

Question: {question}"""

    # [7] GENERATE — grounded answer
    reply = llm.generate(prompt)

    # [8] CITE — hand back the sources for trust/audit
    return reply, [h.metadata["source"] for h in hits]
```

### Read the three lines that matter most

1. `embed(...)` appears **twice on purpose** — questions and documents must live in the **same** meaning-space, or 'nearest' is meaningless. Use the *same embedding model* for both.
2. `"Answer using ONLY the context below... say you don't know."` — your anti-hallucination contract, in two sentences.
3. `return reply, [sources]` — never ship a RAG answer without its receipts.

That's it. No retraining. No frozen knowledge. Just: turn meaning into geometry, fetch the nearest truth, and ask the model to read it.
slides.speakerNotes: Don't teach the API — teach the SHAPE. Point at the two embed() calls and stress 'same model for both, or the geometry doesn't line up.' Re-read the 'ONLY the context... say you don't know' line as the safety contract. End on 'return sources' — receipts build trust. Ask: 'What breaks if I embed my documents with one model and my questions with a different one?' (They're in different coordinate systems — 'nearest' is garbage.)
slides.marketConnection: Higher-level frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack) wrap this exact flow, and managed 'RAG-as-a-service' offerings handle ingestion-to-answer end to end — but in 2026 the prized engineers are the ones who understand what each of these ~30 lines is actually doing.

slides.title: Recap — From Words to Geometry to Grounded Answers
slides.body:
## The mental model you now carry

### The core chain of ideas
1. **Embeddings** turn text into points in a high-dimensional 'meaning space' — similar meaning, nearby points.
2. **Cosine similarity** measures closeness by angle; search becomes 'find the nearest points.'
3. **Vector databases** (FAISS / pgvector / Pinecone-style) store millions of vectors and find neighbors in milliseconds via ANN — with metadata filtering for freshness and access control.
4. **Chunking** is the make-or-break craft: self-contained, meaningful passages with overlap and structure-awareness.
5. **RAG** = ingest (chunk → embed → store), then per query (embed → retrieve → augment → generate → cite).

### The judgment calls
- **Knowledge problems → RAG.** Fresh, citable, permission-aware, cheap to update.
- **Behavior problems → fine-tune.** And the best systems combine both: **RAG for facts, LoRA for voice.**
- **'Answer using ONLY the context'** is the line that turns a guesser into a grounded expert.

### Debugging & trust
- Always ask: **did retrieval fail, or did generation fail?** Log the retrieved chunks.
- A **reranker** is the highest-ROI upgrade for 'right doc, wrong rank.'
- **Evaluate the two stages separately**: context recall/precision for retrieval; faithfulness/relevance for generation. Watch **faithfulness** like a hawk.
- **Always return citations.** Trust is the product.

> If you remember one sentence: **RAG doesn't make the model smarter — it makes the model *informed*, *current*, and *honest about its sources*.**
slides.speakerNotes: Tie the whole arc together: meaning → geometry → retrieval → grounded, cited answers. Make them recite 'did retrieval fail or did generation fail?' — it's the diagnostic reflex that marks a real RAG engineer. Reinforce the Day-10 link: knowledge=RAG, behavior=fine-tune, best systems use both. Then set up the cliffhanger: so far the AI only *reads* and *answers* — tomorrow it starts to *act*.
slides.marketConnection: RAG is the most commercially important pattern in applied AI as of June 2026 — the architecture behind the majority of deployed enterprise assistants — and fluency in embeddings, chunking, retrieval, and RAG evaluation is now a baseline expectation for AI engineers and a frequent interview topic.

slides.title: Quick Quiz — Lock It In
slides.body:
## Six questions. Reason them out before you read the answer.

**1. What is an embedding, in one sentence?**
> A list of numbers (a vector) that represents a piece of text as a point in a high-dimensional 'meaning space,' where similar meanings sit close together.

**2. Why must you embed your documents and your questions with the same model?**
> So they share the same coordinate system. 'Nearest neighbor' only means something if both live in the same meaning-space; mismatched models make 'closeness' meaningless.

**3. A user asks 'how do I get my money back?' but your document says 'refund policy.' No shared keywords. How does RAG still find it?**
> Through **semantic** similarity — the embeddings of 'get my money back' and 'refund' land near each other in meaning-space, so cosine similarity is high despite zero word overlap. (This is why semantic beats keyword search.)

**4. Your RAG bot gives a wrong answer. What's the FIRST thing you check?**
> Whether **retrieval or generation** failed — log the retrieved chunks. If the right chunk wasn't retrieved, fix chunking/embeddings/reranking. If it was retrieved but ignored, fix the prompt/generation.

**5. Which single metric best detects hallucination in a RAG answer?**
> **Faithfulness / groundedness** — whether every claim in the answer is actually supported by the retrieved context.

**6. Why does RAG beat fine-tuning for company knowledge that changes monthly?**
> Freshness (re-embed a changed doc in seconds), traceability (citations you can audit), cheap updates, and access control at retrieval time — fine-tuning bakes facts in, so they go stale and can't be cited.

### One to design
Sketch a RAG system for a hospital that must answer from the *latest* clinical guidelines and *prove* which guideline it cited, while never showing a nurse a doctor-only document. Name the component that handles each requirement. *(Hint: re-ingestion for freshness, citations for proof, metadata filtering for access.)*
slides.speakerNotes: Run as table competition. Questions 3 and 4 are the discriminators — #3 proves they understand semantic vs keyword, #4 proves they can debug. Spend time on the hospital design challenge; have tables map each requirement to a component (freshness→re-ingestion, proof→citations, access→metadata filter). It rehearses real architecture and sets a serious, high-stakes tone that bridges into agents tomorrow.
slides.marketConnection: These are exactly the questions asked in 2026 AI-engineering interviews and enterprise vendor evaluations — semantic vs keyword, retrieval-vs-generation debugging, faithfulness, and access-controlled retrieval are the literacy tests of applied AI.

keyTakeaways: An embedding turns text into a point in high-dimensional 'meaning space' — similar meaning lands nearby — so semantic search becomes geometry (nearest neighbors via cosine similarity).
RAG = ingest (chunk → embed → store in a vector DB), then per query (embed → retrieve top-k → augment the prompt → generate → cite) — all without ever retraining the model.
Chunking is the unglamorous step that makes or breaks RAG: aim for self-contained, meaningful passages with overlap and structure-awareness.
RAG beats fine-tuning for knowledge because it's fresh, citable, cheap to update, and access-controllable; the dream architecture is RAG for facts + LoRA for behavior.
Debug by asking 'did retrieval or generation fail?' — log the chunks; add a reranker for 'right doc, wrong rank'; and enforce 'answer ONLY from context, else say you don't know.'
Evaluate retrieval (context recall/precision) and generation (faithfulness/relevance) separately, with a golden set and LLM-as-judge — and watch faithfulness as your hallucination meter.
marketSnapshot2026: As of June 2026, RAG is the most widely deployed AI architecture in the enterprise — the engine behind customer-support assistants, internal knowledge bots, and legal, financial, and medical copilots. Embedding models are a cheap commodity; the action has moved 'up the stack' to ingestion quality (smart document parsing and chunking), retrieval quality (hybrid search plus rerankers like Cohere Rerank), and rigorous evaluation (RAGAS, TruLens, LLM-as-judge pipelines tracking faithfulness and recall). On infrastructure, pgvector has become the pragmatic default for teams already on Postgres, while managed vector databases (Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus) own large-scale and low-ops deployments, with FAISS still ruling prototyping. Crucially, in regulated industries RAG's citations and access-controlled retrieval are compliance requirements, not features — 'what's your faithfulness score and can it cite its sources?' is now a standard procurement question. RAG literacy is a baseline expectation for AI engineers.
homework: Take the task you brought from Day 10 (or pick a real one) and design a RAG system for it on one page: (1) What documents do you ingest, and how would you chunk them (size, overlap, what natural boundaries)? (2) Which vector DB would you choose — FAISS, pgvector, or managed — and why? (3) Write your exact augmentation prompt, including the 'use ONLY the context' contract. (4) Name one likely failure mode and how you'd detect it (which metric) and fix it. Bonus: if your task needs BOTH fresh facts and a specific voice, describe how you'd combine RAG with a LoRA adapter.
nextDayTeaser: So far our AI can read and answer. Tomorrow it learns to ACT. We cross from chatbots to doers: tool and function calling, the ReAct loop where a model reasons then takes an action then observes the result, multi-step planning, and the agent frameworks racing to orchestrate it all. We'll also meet MCP — the Model Context Protocol that's becoming the universal standard for how models talk to tools and data. This is where the agentic era begins.
